subject: physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

questions.question: The threshold wavelength for a metal having work function $W_0$ is $\lambda_0$. What is the threshold wavelength for a metal whose work function is $\frac{W_0}{2}$?
questions.explanation: The work function is inversely proportional to the threshold wavelength, as given by $W_0 = \frac{hc}{\lambda_0}$. If the work function is halved to $\frac{W_0}{2}$, the threshold wavelength must double to $2\lambda_0$ to satisfy the equation.

questions.question: Which of the following has the longest de-Broglie wavelength if they are moving with same velocity?
questions.explanation: The de-Broglie wavelength is given by $\lambda = \frac{h}{mv}$. For particles moving with the same velocity, the wavelength is inversely proportional to the mass; since the $\beta$-particle (electron) has the smallest mass among the options, it has the longest wavelength.

questions.question: When a point source of light is at a distance of $50\text{ cm}$ from a photoelectric cell, the stopping voltage is found to be $V_0$. If the same source is placed at a distance of $1\text{ m}$ from the cell, the stopping voltage will be
questions.explanation: Stopping voltage depends only on the frequency of the incident light and the nature of the cathode material, not on the intensity. Changing the distance of the light source only affects the intensity (number of photons), so the stopping voltage remains $V_0$.

questions.question: The wavelength of the matter waves is independent of
questions.explanation: According to the de-Broglie hypothesis, the wavelength of matter waves is $\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{mv}$. This expression depends on Planck's constant, mass, velocity, and momentum, but does not involve the electric charge of the particle.

questions.question: The photoelectric threshold wavelength for Potassium having work function of 2 eV, is ........ (Take $h = 6.6 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$ ; $1 \text{ eV} = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$ ; $C = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ ms}^{-1}$)
questions.explanation: The threshold wavelength is calculated using $\lambda_0 = \frac{hc}{W_0}$. Substituting $h = 6.6 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$, $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$, and $W_0 = 2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$, we get $\lambda_0 \approx 618.7 \text{ nm}$.

questions.question: A Photon of energy 8 eV is incident on a metal surface of threshold frequency $1.6 \times 10^{15} \text{ Hz}$. The maximum Kinetic energy of photo electrons emitted is ...... (Take $h = 6.6 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$ ; $1 \text{ eV} = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$)
questions.explanation: Einstein's photoelectric equation is $K_{\text{max}} = E - hf_0$. Calculating the work function $W_0 = hf_0 \approx 6.6 \text{ eV}$ and subtracting it from the incident energy of $8 \text{ eV}$ gives a maximum kinetic energy of $1.4 \text{ eV}$.

questions.question: An $\alpha$ particle and a deuteron are moving with velocities v and 2v respectively. What will be the ratio of their de-Broglie wavelengths ?
questions.explanation: The de-Broglie wavelength is $\lambda = \frac{h}{mv}$. For an $\alpha$ particle ($m \approx 4u$) at velocity $v$, $\lambda_{\alpha} \propto \frac{1}{4v}$, and for a deuteron ($m \approx 2u$) at velocity $2v$, $\lambda_d \propto \frac{1}{2(2v)} = \frac{1}{4v}$, resulting in a $1:1$ ratio.

questions.question: Ultraviolet light by wavelength 200 nm is incident on polished surface of Fe (Iron). Work function of the surface is 4.71 eV. What will be its stopping potential ? ($h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$ ; $1 \text{ eV} = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$ ; $C = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ ms}^{-1}$)
questions.explanation: First, calculate the energy of the incident photon using $E = \frac{hc}{\lambda} \approx 6.21 \text{ eV}$. Using Einstein's equation $eV_0 = E - W_0$, we find $eV_0 = 6.21 - 4.71 = 1.5 \text{ eV}$, which gives a stopping potential of $1.5 \text{ V}$.

questions.question: The number of Photons of wavelength 660 nm emitted per second by an electric bulb of 60 W is ..... (Take $h = 6.6 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$)
questions.explanation: The number of photons emitted per second is $n = \frac{P}{E}$, where $E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$ is the energy of one photon. Substituting the given values, $E = 3 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$, so $n = \frac{60}{3 \times 10^{-19}} = 2 \times 10^{20}$ photons per second.

questions.question: If the linear momentum of a particle is $2.2 \times 10^4 \text{ Kg ms}^{-1}$, then what will be its de-Broglie wavelength ? (Take $h = 6.6 \times 10^{-34} \text{ Js.}$)
questions.explanation: The de-Broglie wavelength is calculated using the formula $\lambda = \frac{h}{p}$. By substituting $h = 6.6 \times 10^{-34} \text{ Js}$ and the given momentum $p$, the wavelength is found to be $3 \times 10^{-29} \text{ m}$ (assuming the power in the question was intended to match the options).

questions.question: In photoelectric effect, the threshold wavelength of Sodium is $5000 \text{ \AA}$. Find its work function. $[ h = 6.6 \times 10^{-34} \text{ Js.} , c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ ms}^{-1} , 1 \text{ eV} = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} ]$
questions.explanation: The work function is given by $W_0 = \frac{hc}{\lambda_0}$. Using the relation $W_0 (\text{eV}) = \frac{12400}{\lambda_0 (\text{\AA})}$, we get $W_0 = \frac{12400}{5000} = 2.48 \text{ eV}$, which is approximately $2.5 \text{ eV}$.

questions.question: An electron is accelerated under a potential difference of $182\text{ V}$. The maximum velocity of electron will be ........... (Charge of electron is $1.6 \times 10^{-19}\text{ C}$ and its mass is $9.1 \times 10^{-31}\text{ kg}$ )
questions.explanation: The maximum velocity of an accelerated electron is found by equating kinetic energy to electric potential energy, $\frac{1}{2}mv^2 = eV$. Solving for $v = \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m}}$ and substituting the given values yields $v = 8 \times 10^6 \text{ m/s}$.

questions.question: An electron and a proton are moving in the same direction with same kinetic energy. The ratio of the De Broglie wavelength associated with these particles is ...........
questions.explanation: The de-Broglie wavelength in terms of kinetic energy $K$ is $\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2mK}}$. Since both particles have the same kinetic energy, the ratio of their wavelengths $\frac{\lambda_e}{\lambda_p}$ is equal to $\sqrt{\frac{m_p}{m_e}}$.

questions.question: A photosensitive metallic surface has work function $\phi$. If photon of energy $3\text{ }\phi$ fall on this surface, the electron comes out with a maximum velocity of $6 \times 10^6\text{ m/s}$. When the photon energy is increased to $9\text{ }\phi$, then maximum velocity of photoelectron will be ........
questions.explanation: From Einstein's equation, $K_{\text{max}} = E - \phi$. In the first case, $K_1 = 3\phi - \phi = 2\phi$, and in the second, $K_2 = 9\phi - \phi = 8\phi$; since $K \propto v^2$, the velocity increases by a factor of $\sqrt{8\phi/2\phi} = 2$, resulting in $12 \times 10^6 \text{ m/s}$.

questions.question: The frequency of a Photon having energy $100\text{ eV}$ is ....... Hz. (Take $h = 6.62 \times 10^{-34}\text{ Js ; } 1\text{ eV} = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\text{ J}$)
questions.explanation: The frequency of a photon is related to its energy by the formula $E = hf$. Converting the energy to Joules ($100 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$) and dividing by Planck's constant $h$ gives a frequency of $2.417 \times 10^{16} \text{ Hz}$.

questions.question: A body of mass $100 \text{ g}$ moves at the speed of $36 \text{ km/hr}$. The de Broglie wave length related to it is of the order ________ $\text{ m}$ ($h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ Js}$)
questions.explanation: The de Broglie wavelength is calculated using the formula $\lambda = \frac{h}{mv}$. Converting speed to SI units ($36 \text{ km/hr} = 10 \text{ m/s}$) and mass to kg ($100 \text{ g} = 0.1 \text{ kg}$), we get $\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{0.1 \times 10} = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ m}$. Thus, the order of magnitude is $10^{-34} \text{ m}$.

questions.question: If the kinetic energy of free electron is made double, the new de Broglie wave length will be ________ times that of initial wave length.
questions.explanation: The de Broglie wavelength is related to kinetic energy $K$ by the formula $\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2mK}}$. If the kinetic energy is doubled ($K' = 2K$), the new wavelength becomes $\lambda' = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2m(2K)}} = \frac{\lambda}{\sqrt{2}}$. Therefore, the wavelength becomes $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ times the initial value.

questions.question: Threshold wave length for lithium metal is $6250 \ \mathring{\text{A}}$. For photo emission, the wave length of the incident light must be ________.
questions.explanation: For photoelectric emission to occur, the energy of incident light must be greater than or equal to the work function ($E \ge \Phi$). Since energy is inversely proportional to wavelength ($E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$), the incident wavelength must be less than or equal to the threshold wavelength. Thus, $\lambda \le 6250 \ \mathring{\text{A}}$.

questions.question: If alpha particle and deutron move with velocity $v$ and $2v$ respectively, the ratio of their de - Broglie wave length will be ________.
questions.explanation: The de Broglie wavelength is given by $\lambda = \frac{h}{mv}$. For an alpha particle ($m_\alpha = 4u$) with velocity $v$, $\lambda_\alpha = \frac{h}{4uv}$, and for a deuteron ($m_d = 2u$) with velocity $2v$, $\lambda_d = \frac{h}{2u(2v)} = \frac{h}{4uv}$. Comparing the two, the ratio $\lambda_\alpha : \lambda_d$ is $1:1$.

questions.question: de - Broglie wave length of atom at $T$ K absolute temperature will be
questions.explanation: The average kinetic energy of an atom at absolute temperature $T$ is $K = \frac{3}{2}kT$, where $k$ is the Boltzmann constant. Substituting this into the de-Broglie wavelength formula $\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2mK}}$, we get $\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2m(\frac{3}{2}kT)}} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{3mkT}}$.

questions.question: Photons having energy $1\text{ eV}$ and $2.5\text{ eV}$ successively incident on a metal having work function is $0.5\text{ eV}$. The ratio of maximum speed of emitted electrons is
questions.explanation: Using Einstein's photoelectric equation $K_{max} = E - \Phi$, the maximum kinetic energies are $K_1 = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5 \text{ eV}$ and $K_2 = 2.5 - 0.5 = 2.0 \text{ eV}$. Since $K_{max} = \frac{1}{2}mv_{max}^2$, the ratio of speeds is $\frac{v_1}{v_2} = \sqrt{\frac{K_1}{K_2}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.5}{2.0}} = \frac{1}{2}$.

questions.question: In experiment of Davisson-Germer, emitted electron from filament is accelerated through voltage $V$ then de-Broglie wavelength of that electron will be _____ m.
questions.explanation: In the Davisson-Germer experiment, electrons are accelerated through a potential $V$, gaining kinetic energy $K = eV$. The de-Broglie wavelength is then calculated using the relation $\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2mK}}$. Substituting $K = eV$, we obtain $\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2meV}}$.

questions.question: Photons of energy 1 eV and 2.5 eV successively illuminated a metal whose work function is 0.5 eV. The ratio of maximum speeds of emitted electron is
questions.explanation: According to Einstein's photoelectric equation, $K_{max} = E - \Phi$. For the two cases, $K_1 = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5 \text{ eV}$ and $K_2 = 2.5 - 0.5 = 2.0 \text{ eV}$. The ratio of maximum speeds is $\frac{v_1}{v_2} = \sqrt{\frac{K_1}{K_2}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.5}{2.0}} = \frac{1}{2}$.

questions.question: If the uncertainty in the position of an electron is $10^{-10}\text{ m}$, then the value of uncertainty in its momentum will be ___ $\text{kgms}^{-1}$. ($h = 6.62 \times 10^{-34}\text{ J-s}$)
questions.explanation: According to Heisenberg's Uncertainty Principle, $\Delta x \Delta p \ge \frac{h}{4\pi}$, but often the relation $\Delta x \Delta p \approx \frac{h}{2\pi}$ is used in textbook problems. Calculating $\Delta p = \frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34}}{2 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-10}}$, we get approximately $1.05 \times 10^{-24} \text{ kg m/s}$.

questions.question: If the energy of photons corresponding to wavelength of $6000\text{ Å}$ is $3.2 \times 10^{-19}\text{ J}$. The photon energy for wavelength of $4000\text{ Å}$ will be ___
questions.explanation: The energy of a photon is inversely proportional to its wavelength, $E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$. Given $E_1$ for $\lambda_1 = 6000 \text{ }$, the energy $E_2$ for $\lambda_2 = 4000 \text{ }$ is calculated as $E_2 = E_1 \times \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2} = 3.2 \times 10^{-19} \times \frac{6000}{4000} = 4.8 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$.

questions.question: ____ is the wavelength of photon of energy $35\text{ KeV}$. $h = 6.625 \times 10^{-34}\text{ J-s}$, $c = 3 \times 10^8\text{ m/s}$, $1\text{ eV} = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\text{ J}$.
questions.explanation: Using $\lambda = \frac{hc}{E}$, we substitute $E = 35 \times 10^3 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$. This gives $\lambda \approx 3.5 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m} = 35 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}$. Option C is the closest match, assuming the unit 'mm' is a typographical error for 'm'.

questions.question: In X-ray tube the potential difference between the anode and the cathode is $20 \text{ kV}$ and the current flowing is $1.6 \text{ mA}$. The number of electrons striking the anode in $1 \text{ s}$ is............ (Charge of an electron = $1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$)
questions.explanation: The current $I$ is the rate of flow of charge, $I = \frac{ne}{t}$. Given $I = 1.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}$ and $t = 1 \text{ s}$, the number of electrons $n$ is calculated as $n = \frac{I}{e} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-3}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = 10^{16}$.

questions.question: Photons of energy $2\text{eV}$ and $2.5 \text{ eV}$ successively illuminate a metal whose work function is $0.5 \text{ eV}$. The ratio of maximum speed of emitted electron is...........
questions.explanation: From Einstein's photoelectric equation $K_{max} = E - \Phi$, the kinetic energies are $K_1 = 2 - 0.5 = 1.5 \text{ eV}$ and $K_2 = 2.5 - 0.5 = 2.0 \text{ eV}$. The ratio of maximum speeds is $\frac{v_1}{v_2} = \sqrt{\frac{K_1}{K_2}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.5}{2.0}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$.

questions.question: To increase de Broglie wavelength of an electron from $0.5 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}$ to $10^{-10} \text{ m}$, its energy should be.............
questions.explanation: The de Broglie wavelength is related to energy by $\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2mE}}$, which implies $E \propto \frac{1}{\lambda^2}$. To increase the wavelength from $0.5 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}$ to $10^{-10} \text{ m}$ (a factor of 2), the energy must be changed by a factor of $(\frac{1}{2})^2 = \frac{1}{4}$.

questions.question: How much is the De-Broglie wavelength for an electron accelerated by an $100\text{ V}$ potential difference?
questions.explanation: For an electron accelerated through a potential difference $V$, the de Broglie wavelength is given by $\lambda = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{V}} \text{ nm}$. Substituting $V = 100 \text{ V}$, we get $\lambda = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{100}} = 0.1227 \text{ nm}$, which is approximately $0.123 \text{ nm}$.

questions.question: The threshold frequency of cesium is $5.16 \times 10^{14}\text{ Hz}$. Then its work function is ...... $\text{eV}$.
questions.explanation: The work function $\phi_0$ is calculated using the formula $\phi_0 = h\nu_0$, where $\nu_0$ is the threshold frequency. Substituting the given values, $\phi_0 = (6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 5.16 \times 10^{14}) / (1.6 \times 10^{-19}) \approx 2.14\text{ eV}$. This direct application of the formula confirms option A.

questions.question: Which of those metal having least work function ($\phi_0$) among them?
questions.explanation: Alkali metals like Sodium ($Na$) have the lowest work functions because their single valence electron is relatively far from the nucleus and loosely bound. Among the given options, Sodium ($Na$) requires the least energy to emit an electron from its surface. This is a standard conceptual fact in the study of the photoelectric effect.

questions.question: What is the de-Broglie wavelength associated with an electron, accelerated through a potential difference of $64\text{ volts}$? $[h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34}\text{ J.s}]$
questions.explanation: The de-Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated through a potential $V$ is given by the simplified formula $\lambda = \frac{12.27}{\sqrt{V}} \text{ \AA}$. For $V = 64\text{ V}$, the calculation becomes $\lambda = \frac{12.27}{8} \approx 1.53 \text{ \AA}$. Thus, option C is the correct result.

questions.question: In photoelectrical effect, that the graph of stopping potential ($V_0$) versus frequency $\nu$ is straight line. What will be the slope of this straight line?
questions.explanation:
Einstein's photoelectric equation is $eV_0 = h
u - \phi_0$, which can be rearranged into the slope-intercept form $V_0 = ( rac{h}{e})
u -  rac{\phi_0}{e}$. In a graph of stopping potential ($V_0$) versus frequency ($
u$), the slope is represented by the constant term $ rac{h}{e}$. This makes option C the correct choice.

questions.question: What is the de-Broglie wavelength associated with an electron moving with a speed of $6.4 \times 10^6\text{ m/s}$? [Mass of electron $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}\text{ kg}$, Planck's constant $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34}\text{ J.s.}$]
questions.explanation: The de-Broglie wavelength is determined by the formula $\lambda = \frac{h}{mv}$. Substituting the mass of the electron $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}\text{ kg}$ and the velocity $v = 6.4 \times 10^6\text{ m/s}$, we get $\lambda \approx 0.114 \times 10^{-9}\text{ m}$. This value corresponds to $0.114\text{ nm}$, which is option B.

questions.question: An electron, an $\alpha$-particle and a proton have the same kinetic energy. Which of these particles has the shortest de-Broglie wavelength?
questions.explanation: The de-Broglie wavelength is related to kinetic energy $K$ by $\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2mK}}$. For the same kinetic energy, the wavelength is inversely proportional to the square root of the mass ($\lambda \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{m}}$). Since the $\alpha$-particle has the largest mass among the given particles, it will have the shortest wavelength.

questions.question: What is the de Broglie wavelength associated with an electron accelerated through a potential difference of 64 volts?
questions.explanation: Using the formula $\lambda = \frac{12.27}{\sqrt{V}} \text{ \AA}$, for a potential difference of $64\text{ V}$, we calculate $\lambda = \frac{12.27}{8} \approx 1.53 \text{ \AA}$. Although the provided key indicates B, the standard calculation for $64\text{ V}$ results in $1.53 \text{ \AA}$, which is option C.

questions.question: An electron, an $\alpha$-particle and a proton have the same kinetic energy. Which of these have longest de Broglie wavelength?
questions.explanation: The de-Broglie wavelength is given by $\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2mK}}$. For particles with the same kinetic energy $K$, the wavelength is inversely proportional to the square root of their mass. Because the electron has the smallest mass compared to a proton or an $\alpha$-particle, it possesses the longest de-Broglie wavelength.

questions.question: The number of photons emitted per second by a bulb of 66 W power emitting waves of wavelength 600 nm is ________. $(h = 6.6 \times 10^{-34} \text{J.s})$
questions.explanation: The number of photons emitted per second is $n = \frac{P}{E} = \frac{P\lambda}{hc}$. Substituting $P = 66\text{ W}$, $\lambda = 600 \times 10^{-9}\text{ m}$, and the given constants, we get $n = \frac{66 \times 600 \times 10^{-9}}{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8} = 2 \times 10^{20}$. Therefore, option D is the correct answer.

questions.question: To emit an electron from the metal, minimum electric field required is ________.
questions.explanation: Field emission is the process where electrons are pulled out of a metal by a very strong external electric field. The typical magnitude required for this to occur is approximately $10^8\text{ Vm}^{-1}$. This high field strength is necessary to sufficiently lower the potential barrier at the surface, making option D correct.

questions.question: A ball of mass $0.12\text{ kg}$ moving with a speed of $20\text{ ms}^{-1}$ has de-Broglie wavelength ($h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34}\text{ Js}$)
questions.explanation: The de-Broglie wavelength for a macroscopic object is $\lambda = \frac{h}{mv}$. For a ball with $m = 0.12\text{ kg}$ and $v = 20\text{ ms}^{-1}$, the wavelength is $\lambda = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{0.12 \times 20} = 2.7625 \times 10^{-34}\text{ m}$. This matches option C.

questions.question: The minimum value of electric field required to pulled out electrons from a metal is approximately ________ V/cm.
questions.explanation: Field emission requires an electric field of the order of $10^8\text{ V/m}$. Converting this to V/cm, we get $10^8\text{ V} / 100\text{ cm} = 10^6\text{ V/cm}$. While the provided answer is A ($10^9$), the standard physical requirement for field emission is typically cited as $10^6$ to $10^8\text{ V/cm}$ depending on the surface geometry.

questions.question: Monochromatic light of frequency $6 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}$ is produced by a laser. The power emitted is $4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W}$. How many photons per second on an average are emitted by the source? [$h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ Js}$]
questions.explanation: The number of photons emitted per second is calculated by $n = \frac{P}{h\nu}$. Using $P = 4 \times 10^{-3}\text{ W}$ and $\nu = 6 \times 10^{14}\text{ Hz}$, we find $n = \frac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 6 \times 10^{14}} \approx 1 \times 10^{16}$. This confirms option A as the correct choice.

questions.question: What is the de-Broglie wavelength of a bullet of mass 0.033 kg travelling at the speed of 1 km/s? ($h = 6.6 \times 10^{-34} \text{ Js}$)
questions.explanation: The de-Broglie wavelength is $\lambda = \frac{h}{mv}$. For a bullet with $m = 0.033\text{ kg}$ and $v = 1000\text{ m/s}$, the momentum $p = 33\text{ kg.m/s}$. Thus, $\lambda = \frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34}}{33} = 0.2 \times 10^{-34} = 2 \times 10^{-35}\text{ m}$, which is option B.
